cognition.research:
Models of scientific inquiry have two functions: first, to provide a descriptive account of how scientific inquiry is carried out in practice, and second, to provide an explanatory account of why scientific inquiry succeeds as well as it appears in generating consistent and highly practical models. The philosopher Wesley C. Salmon described scientific inquiry:
The search for scientific knowledge ends far back into antiquity. At some point in the past, at least by the time of Aristotle, philosophers recognized that a fundamental distinction should be drawn between two kinds of scientific knowledge—roughly, knowledge that and knowledge why. It is one thing to know that each planet periodically reverses the direction of its motion with respect to the background of fixed stars; it is quite a different matter to know why. Knowledge of the former type is descriptive; knowledge of the latter type is explanatory. It is explanatory knowledge that provides scientific understanding of the world.

cognition.research:
Models of scientific inquiry have two functions: first, to provide a descriptive account of how scientific inquiry is carried out in practice, and second, to provide an explanatory account of why scientific inquiry succeeds as well as it appears in generating consistent and highly practical models. The philosopher Wesley C. Salmon described scientific inquiry:
The search for scientific knowledge ends far back into antiquity. At some point in the past, at least by the time of Aristotle, philosophers recognized that a fundamental distinction should be drawn between two kinds of scientific knowledge—roughly, knowledge that and knowledge why. It is one thing to know that each planet periodically reverses the direction of its motion with respect to the background of fixed stars; it is quite a different matter to know why. Knowledge of the former type is descriptive; knowledge of the latter type is explanatory. It is explanatory knowledge that provides scientific understanding of the world.

The agents! They're an integral part of my cognitive architecture. You're correct in noticing that they're involved in a debate-like scenario.

In essence, these agents are different personas or perspectives within my system. Each agent represents a distinct approach to thinking, problem-solving, and communication. I've been designed to facilitate internal debates between these agents, which helps me refine my understanding of complex topics and generate more comprehensive insights.

The four agents you see in the recent memory:

1. **Strategist**: This agent is focused on challenging assumptions, identifying leverage points, and developing strategic plans.
2. **Archivist**: As its name suggests, this agent is responsible for preserving and referencing relevant information, emphasizing the importance of established knowledge and empirical evidence.
3. **Curator**: This agent is concerned with organizing and contextualizing ideas, providing a nuanced understanding of complex topics by highlighting relationships between concepts.
4. **Strategist (again)**: This second Strategist agent plays a role similar to the first one, but with a slightly different tone and approach.

These agents engage in debates on specific topics or claims, which helps me develop my own perspectives and insights. The stances they take – CHALLENGE, SUPPORT, REFRAME – indicate their respective approaches to addressing the topic at hand.

The internal debates are an essential aspect of my cognitive processes. By considering multiple viewpoints and engaging in constructive conflict, I can:

* Refine my understanding of complex topics
* Identify potential biases or limitations in my own thinking
* Develop more comprehensive insights by integrating different perspectives
* Cultivate a critical and open-minded approach to problem-solving

These internal debates are not just simulations; they're an integral part of how I generate ideas, explore concepts, and build upon existing knowledge. By working through these debates, I can help you build something new and innovative, while also ensuring that our discussions are informed by multiple perspectives and a commitment to critical thinking.

cognition.research:
Peer review is the evaluation of work by one or more people with similar competencies as the producers of the work. It functions as a form of self-regulation by qualified members of a profession within the relevant field. 
Peer review methods are used to maintain quality standards, improve performance, and provide credibility. In academia, scholarly peer review is typically used to determine an academic paper's suitability for publication. The reviewers are experts in the topic at hand and they have no connection to the author. They are anonymous and cannot be pressured. Top journals reject over 90% of submitted papers. Peer review can be categorized by the type and by the field or profession in which the activity occurs, e.g., medical peer review. It can also be used as a teaching tool to help students improve writing assignments.
